Design System
🎨Design System
Relowa POC · v0.1
Home / ADR-0008

ADR-0008 — Arbitrum One for Daily Merkle Root Anchoring

StatusAccepted
Date2026-05-09
Decision-makersOzan (lead)

Context

Relowa's audit architecture already has two tamper-detection layers:

  1. Hash chain on audit_events — each row chains to its predecessor via SHA-256, detecting row deletion or modification.
  2. S3 WORM mirror — daily JSON-Lines export to S3 with Object Lock, providing a legal-evidence backstop independent of the database.

Neither of these proves when a record was created in a way that an external auditor can independently verify without trusting our infrastructure. An adversarial scenario: we could backdate rows in the DB, recompute the hash chain forward, and upload a fake S3 mirror — an auditor with only our word couldn't tell.

We need a trust-minimized timestamp: a single 32-byte fingerprint published somewhere no single party controls, proving "at block height H, this exact set of records existed."

🇪🇺

Three EU regulations make this valuable:

  • CSRD (Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive) — verifiable supply-chain emissions data.
  • ESPR (Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation) — digital product passport with auditable provenance.
  • WSR (Waste Shipment Regulation) — cross-border waste tracking with integrity guarantees.

This is a compliance primitive, not a blockchain product. No tokens, no DeFi, no business logic on-chain.

Decision

We anchor a daily Merkle root to Arbitrum One via a scheduled Lambda. Once per day:

  1. Compute a Merkle tree over audit_events rows from the last 24h.
  2. Compute a Merkle tree over material_recovery_certificates from the last 24h.
  3. Compute the combined Merkle root: sha256(audit_root || cert_root).
  4. Call the Anchor contract on Arbitrum One: record(bytes32 root).

Gas costs approximately $0.01/day (~$3.65/year).

How Verification Works

Any third party can independently verify:

  1. Obtain the Merkle root from the Arbitrum block explorer for a given date.
  2. Request supporting audit_events rows from Relowa's API.
  3. Recompute the Merkle root client-side.
  4. If recomputed root matches on-chain root → records are authentic and existed at that block height.

Why not more than a Merkle root: Publishing individual event hashes on-chain would be ~100× more expensive and risk KVKK/GDPR issues. A single Merkle root has constant cost regardless of event volume, zero data exposure (root reveals nothing about underlying data), and is sufficient for all three EU regulations.

Consequences

▲ Positive
  • Third-party-verifiable timestamp without trusting Relowa's infrastructure.
  • Negligible cost (~$0.01/day ≈ $3.65/year).
  • EVM-compatible — standard tooling (ethers.js, viem).
  • Arbitrum One → Ethereum L1 security after 7-day challenge window.
  • Regulatory optionality for future EU requirements.
▼ Negative
  • New AWS Lambda + EventBridge Scheduler to maintain.
  • One-time contract deploy (minimal, single-function).
  • Operational dependency on anchor Lambda — mitigated by CloudWatch alarm.

Alternatives Considered

Chain Decision Rationale
Polygon PoS Rejected Centralized validator set, multi-sig bridge, reorg history.
Ethereum L1 Rejected $1–5/day for a single 32-byte write is unreasonable.
Base Viable Coinbase backing; Arbitrum has stronger decentralization track record.
Linea Viable zkEVM finality; younger chain with less battle-testing.
Optimism Rejected Same tech as Base but weaker institutional brand.
Solana Rejected Non-EVM, incompatible with our toolchain.
Gnosis Rejected L1 sidechain, no Ethereum settlement.
Bitcoin OP_RETURN Rejected Better trust-root but pricier/slower, no EVM.
Arbitrum One Selected Leading optimistic rollup — strongest decentralization, lowest cost, EVM, Ethereum settlement.

The Anchor Contract

// SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
pragma solidity ^0.8.24;

contract AuditAnchor {
    event RootRecorded(
        bytes32 indexed root,
        uint256 indexed blockNumber,
        uint256 timestamp
    );

    function record(bytes32 root) external {
        emit RootRecorded(root, block.number, block.timestamp);
    }
}

Non-upgradeable. Single-function. Deployed once. No storage — emits events only. The events become part of Arbitrum's state, which settles to Ethereum L1 after a 7-day challenge window.

See Also
